title: From Research to Production
concept: Deploying a vision application in production means going beyond a research demo — handling edge cases, monitoring performance over time, managing latency budgets, and planning for graceful failure.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why doesn't running two completely unrelated models on the same codebase count as a real 'vision pipeline'?

options.text: A real pipeline requires structured hand-offs where one stage's output deliberately feeds the next stage's input
Any two models sharing a codebase automatically form a pipeline
Pipelines require exactly zero models to function

exercises.explain: Yes! True pipelines need deliberate, structured stage connections.

cards.title: A demo that works once vs. a system that works always
cards.body: Getting a face recognition model to correctly identify someone in a nice, well-lit demo photo is one milestone. Getting that same system to work reliably for millions of real users, in poor lighting, at 2am, on a budget camera, is an entirely different engineering challenge.

cards.title: Research-to-Production Considerations
cards.body: Moving a vision application to production requires: handling edge cases (unusual lighting, angles, occlusion), monitoring performance drift over time (does accuracy degrade as the real world changes?), managing a strict latency budget (callback to TTA tradeoffs from Unit 131), and planning graceful failure (what happens when the model is uncertain or wrong?).

cards.title: A recipe that works in your kitchen vs. a restaurant that serves thousands nightly
cards.body: A chef perfecting one dish in their own kitchen, with full control and time, is very different from a restaurant reliably serving that same dish, correctly, to thousands of different customers every single night, under time pressure, with ingredient variability. Production deployment is the restaurant, not the home kitchen.

cards.title: A hospital deployment checklist
cards.body: Before deploying a medical anomaly detector, a hospital team must verify: does it work across different scanner brands (edge cases)? Is a doctor alerted if the model's confidence is low (graceful failure)? Is accuracy tracked over months to catch any drift (monitoring)? Can it run fast enough during a live patient scan (latency)?

cards.title: A high research benchmark score doesn't guarantee production readiness
cards.body: It's a common and costly mistake to assume a model that scores 98% on a clean research benchmark is automatically ready to ship. Real-world deployment introduces messy edge cases, distribution shift, and operational requirements that a benchmark score alone never reveals — production readiness requires its own dedicated evaluation.

cards.title: Research Demo vs. Production System
cards.body: What changes between the two stages:

left.text:
Works on a curated test set
No latency or scale constraints
Success = high benchmark score

right.text:
Must handle messy real-world edge cases
Strict latency/scale/monitoring needs
Success = reliable at scale, over time

exercises.prompt: What is a key difference between a research demo and a production vision system?

options.text: Production systems must handle real-world edge cases, latency constraints, and long-term monitoring that a research demo doesn't need to address
Production systems never need to be tested at all
Research demos require more GPUs than production systems
There is no meaningful difference between the two

exercises.explain: Correct! Production deployment introduces operational demands beyond a clean research benchmark.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model scoring 98% accuracy on a clean research benchmark is automatically guaranteed to be ready for production deployment.
exercises.explain: False. Real-world deployment introduces edge cases and operational requirements a benchmark score alone doesn't reveal.

exercises.sentence: Tracking whether a deployed model's accuracy degrades over time as the real world changes is called performance ___.
exercises.explain: Monitoring tracks whether a model's real-world performance drifts or degrades after deployment.

exercises.prompt: Why must a hospital verify a medical anomaly detector works across different scanner brands before deployment, even if it scored well in research testing?

options.text: Different scanner brands represent real-world edge cases the research benchmark may not have fully covered, risking degraded performance in practice
Scanner brand has no effect on any vision model's performance
Research benchmarks always perfectly represent every real-world deployment scenario

exercises.explain: Correct! Edge case coverage is a critical production concern beyond benchmark performance.

exercises.prompt: A team celebrates their new defect detector hitting 99% accuracy on their curated research test set, and wants to ship it to the factory floor immediately without further testing. What is the risk?

options.text: The research test set may not reflect real-world edge cases, latency constraints, or monitoring needs the factory deployment will actually face
There is no risk; a 99% research score always guarantees flawless production performance
Research accuracy and production accuracy are mathematically required to always be identical
Shipping immediately is always the correct engineering decision regardless of testing

exercises.explain: Exactly! A strong benchmark score is necessary but not sufficient for production readiness. Next: the final capstone, tying the entire Advanced Vision Applications phase together.
